bool dfs(int node, const vector<vector<int>>& adj, vector<int>& visited) {
   if (visited[node] == 1) {
       // we've found a back edge, cycle detected
       return false;
   }
   if (visited[node] == 2) {
       // already processed, no problem
       return true;
   }
   // mark as visiting
   visited[node] = 1;
   for (int neighbor : adj[node]) {
       if (!dfs(neighbor, adj, visited)) {
           return false;
       }
   }
   // mark as processed
   visited[node] = 2;
   return true;
}

Yes, that makes sense. So, in the helper function, if the node is already in the visiting state (1), it's a cycle. If it's processed (2), it's fine. Otherwise, process it.

Example 2: numCourses=2, prerequisites=[[1,0],[0,1]]

So, starting with node 0: mark as 1, process neighbor 1.

Node 1 is unvisited: mark as 1, process neighbor 0.

Node 0 is already marked as 1, so return false.

Thus, the function returns false.

So, the code should handle these cases correctly.

Now, considering the constraints: numCourses can be up to 2000, and prerequisites up to 5000. The DFS approach should handle this efficiently, as each node is visited once, and each edge is processed once. So the time complexity is O(n + m), where n is numCourses and m is the number of prerequisites.

Space complexity is O(n + m) for the adjacency list and the visited array.

solution:
```cpp
#include <vector>
using namespace std;

class Solution {
public:
    bool canFinish(int numCourses, vector<vector<int>>& prerequisites) {
        vector<vector<int>> adj(numCourses);
        for (auto& p : prerequisites) {
            adj[p[1]].push_back(p[0]);
        }
        vector<int> visited(numCourses, 0);
        
        for (int i = 0; i < numCourses; ++i) {
            if (visited[i] == 0) {
                if (!dfs(i, adj, visited)) {
                    return false;
                }
            }
        }
        return true;
    }
    
private:
    bool dfs(int node, const vector<vector<int>>& adj, vector<int>& visited) {
        if (visited[node] == 1) {
            return false;
        }
        if (visited[node] == 2) {
            return true;
        }
        visited[node] = 1;
        for (int neighbor : adj[node]) {
            if (!dfs(neighbor, adj, visited)) {
                return false;
            }
        }
        visited[node] = 2;
        return true;
    }
};
```

efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(n + m)
space complexity: O(n + m)
energy implications: LOW
explanation: This solution uses Depth-First Search (DFS) to detect cycles in the course prerequisite graph. Each node and edge is processed once, ensuring high efficiency. The approach correctly identifies if all courses can be completed without prerequisites forming a cycle.
